research_summary: Qure.ai is a healthcare AI company ($125M raised, 106 countries, 18 FDA clearances) building clinical AI on imaging + LLMs. The open LLM Engineer role in Bengaluru asks for production RAG, LangChain, AWS, OpenAI/Claude -- Amit's exact stack. Company is in explicit growth phase (new CSGO Apr 2026, LLM Engineer hiring Jan 2026, India AI Summit Feb 2026). Email pattern: first.last@qure.ai (97%). 8 contacts across Tier 1/3/4; CTO skipped per >50-employee rule.
jd_full_text:
Job Title: LLM Engineer at Qure.ai, based out of Bengaluru - Full-Time Role

Company Details: Qure.AI is an applied healthcare AI company solving high stakes problems in global healthcare. With 18 FDA and 62 CE marking clearances to date, we create innovative solutions that significantly improve patient outcomes across various care domains, including lung cancer, tuberculosis, and stroke, impacting ~15 million patients annually in over 100 countries.

Our teams are empowered by a strong infrastructure, state-of-the-art tools, and resources, enabling them to tackle real-world problems efficiently. Each contribution you make becomes part of a global diagnostic network, influencing the lives of over 32 million people.

At Qure AI, we are dedicated to cultivating a diverse and inclusive environment and take pride in being an equal-opportunity employer.

Job Roles & Responsibilities:
- Develop and deploy scalable AI applications using AWS and GCP to enhance healthcare solutions.
- Implement and optimize large language models (LLMs), focusing on tasks like Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and multimodal prompting.
- Integrate OpenAI and Anthropic Claude tools into existing healthcare frameworks for improved diagnostic accuracy.
- Collaborate on open-source projects to innovate health tech solutions.
- Use Docker and LangChain for efficient AI model deployment and management.
- Work closely with cross-functional teams to deliver accessible AI-driven healthcare solutions, ensuring they meet technical and clinical requirements.

Cultural Expectations:
- Collaborate in a diverse team, promoting inclusivity and open-mindedness
- Proactively innovate and think outside the box for AI solutions
- Passion for advancing healthcare accessibility
- Adapt and respond to dynamic challenges with a solutions-oriented mindset
- Commit to lifelong learning and sharing knowledge within the team

Hiring Process:
- Technical assignment tailored for the role.
- Interview with hiring manager to assess technical skills.
- Live coding task to evaluate coding proficiency.
- Technical interview with senior member from the technical team.
- Final interview with Tech Lead/CTO

Skills: aws, gcp, llm, rag, multimodal prompting, openai, anthropic claude, docker, langchain

Salary: 30-50 LPA | Experience: 3+ yrs | Location: India | Type: full-time
tailoring_notes: 1. Summary rewritten to mirror JD language: 'production LLM and RAG systems', 'LangChain/LangGraph pipelines', 'AWS (EC2, Lambda, S3)', 'on-premise document pipelines with zero external API exposure' -- JD explicitly asks for LLMs/RAG, LangChain, AWS, and data-sensitive healthcare context. 2. Technical Skills reordered: LLMs/Techniques (with LangChain, LangGraph, Anthropic Claude, multimodal prompting) moved to #1; Cloud/Infrastructure (AWS, GCP, Docker) moved to #2; Vector DBs to #3; Languages to #4; Hardware last -- JD names AWS, GCP, Docker, LangChain explicitly. 3. Projects: swapped WeepScope (hardware/audio, irrelevant for LLM-infra role) for SAP Analytics Bot (Text-to-SQL + NLP, shows LLM application work not already in work experience). Voicer kept for Samsung Top 10 social proof. Work experience bullets unchanged -- already lead with LangChain/LangGraph agentic RAG at Vivansh and FAISS-to-Milvus migration at Silver Touch.

contacts.research_hook: Director of Engineering at Qure.ai; mentioned alongside Ankit Modi (new CSGO) and Avinash Ranjan in a LinkedIn post about rolling out AI-powered X-ray systems via the MinXray partnership -- leads engineering for clinical AI infrastructure deployed in 106 countries. Ex-Engineering Manager at Everwell Health Solutions.
contacts.subject: clinical AI pipeline architecture at Qure
contacts.content:
Hi Abul,

Caught the MinXray rollout post a few months back. Taking AI-powered X-ray screening to new countries in a single deployment cycle is a genuinely hard infrastructure problem, and I wanted to ask how you are thinking about the LLM layer on top of Qure's existing imaging stack.

For context: I've spent the last year rebuilding RAG infrastructure at scale. At Silver Touch I migrated from FAISS to Milvus, went from 45% to 95% MRR on 50K+ queries/month. At Vivansh I built an agentic LangChain/LangGraph pipeline on top, enforcing 250+ per-user permissions per query with 2-stage Milvus retrieval. Also built a fully on-prem OCR system for sensitive government docs: no cloud API calls, Llama-3.1-Nemotron hosted on the client's own server. The data-sovereignty constraint felt similar to what clinical AI teams deal with.

If your team is adding LLM capacity right now, I'd be glad to spend 15 minutes on a call. Open to Tuesday or Thursday if either works.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | amitgohel.dev/chat
contacts.follow_up_1_subject: re: clinical AI pipeline architecture at Qure
contacts.follow_up_1_content:
Hi Abul,

Bumping this in case it got buried. One thing I've added since my last note: a live demo of the multi-tenant RAG system at amitgohel.dev/chat. Permission filtering and conversation continuity across turns, running on Milvus plus GPT-4o.

Happy to close the loop if the timing isn't right.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai
contacts.follow_up_2_subject: re: clinical AI pipeline architecture at Qure
contacts.follow_up_2_content:
Hi Abul,

Last note from me. If Qure isn't actively adding LLM engineers right now, no worries. Would still appreciate being on your radar for when that changes.

Amit

contacts.research_hook: IIT Bombay grad; tagged by CTO Pradeep Kumar Thummala 10 months ago in a post: 'at Qure AI, you are building systems that ripple across a global healthcare network'; also welcomed as a new joiner by Divya Sharma 5 months ago. Has a personal writing blog at rohithkishan.wordpress.com.
contacts.subject: question about Qure's GenAI stack in production
contacts.content:
Hi Rohith,

Going through your Qure.ai profile this week. You've been building there for a while now and I wanted to ask: what does the actual production LLM stack look like for clinical document processing? The blog posts hint at it but don't get into the retrieval architecture specifics.

I've done two years of this side of things: rebuilt a RAG system from FAISS to Milvus with BGE-M3 after testing six embedding models, got MRR from 45% to 95% at 50K+ queries/month. The more recent work is agentic: LangChain plus LangGraph with 2-stage retrieval enforcing 250+ per-user permissions per query, running on AWS EC2 with GPT-4o. That combination of retrieval depth and access control feels like it belongs in clinical AI workflows.

If you're hiring on the ML/LLM side or know who I should talk to, I'd appreciate a pointer. Open to a 15-min call if useful.

Amit
github.com/amit-gohel | amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | amitgohel.dev/chat

contacts.follow_up_1_content:

Following up. Noticed your blog at rohithkishan.wordpress.com while looking at your profile -- good sign you think in writing. If you ever put something out about the engineering tradeoffs in healthcare AI systems I'd read it.

Separately, live RAG demo at amitgohel.dev/chat if you want to see the permission-aware retrieval in action.

Amit

contacts.subject: connecting imaging AI to LLM retrieval at Qure
contacts.content:
Hi Pranav,

Read about Qure.ai's work on AI-enabled biomarker detection and the closed-loop follow-up system for lung nodule tracking. Running diagnostic AI plus automated clinical documentation in the same workflow is an interesting problem, and I imagine the retrieval layer connecting imaging output to the document side is not trivial.

Background that feels relevant: I rebuilt a production RAG system from FAISS to Milvus (45% to 95% MRR, 50K+ queries/month) and then built an agentic upgrade using LangChain/LangGraph with 2-stage retrieval. The permission-aware filtering layer in that one enforces 250+ permissions per query with no caching possible. Not clinical, but the data sensitivity and retrieval complexity are similar problems.

Is there a 15-min window to talk about how the R&D team thinks about LLM integration on top of the imaging models? Happy to work around your schedule.

Amit
amitgohel.dev | linkedin.com/in/amitgohel-ai | amitgohel.dev/chat

contacts.subject: LLM system design for clinical documentation
contacts.content:
Hi Sahil,

Was reading the Qure.ai India AI Summit recap from February. The point about healthcare AI moving from pilots to population-scale deployment resonates: that's exactly when the LLM retrieval layer stops being a demo problem and starts being a systems problem.

Two years building that side of things: migrated a RAG chatbot from FAISS to Milvus (45% to 95% MRR, 50K+ queries/month), then built an agentic version with LangChain/LangGraph enforcing 250+ per-user permissions per query. Also ran an on-prem OCR pipeline for sensitive docs where nothing left the client server. The constraint of data never leaving the building is something clinical AI teams run into constantly.

If you're thinking about LLM hires on the GenAI side, I'd appreciate 15 minutes. Open to Tuesday or Thursday.

Amit

contacts.subject: Qure AI team and the LLM engineer role
contacts.content:
Hi Vaishnav,

Came across your profile while looking into Qure's AI engineering team. Image processing pipelines at production scale is a different discipline from pure LLM work, and I'm curious how the two sides of the stack interact at Qure: does the imaging layer feed structured output into a retrieval system or does it stay separate?

I've been doing the retrieval and LLM orchestration side for two years: FAISS to Milvus migration (45% to 95% MRR), BGE-M3 plus reranker after benchmarking six models, then LangChain/LangGraph agentic pipelines on AWS with per-user RBAC. More recently an on-prem OCR system that had to run with zero cloud API exposure. The data-sensitivity constraint there overlaps with what clinical AI needs.

If you're open to a quick 15-minute conversation about the team, I'd find that genuinely useful.

Amit
